perturbation.original_text: 1.2. The confidentiality obligations set forth under this Agreement do not extend to Excluded Information. For the purposes of this Agreement, “Excluded Information” means information that the Receiving Party can demonstrate (i) was known to the Receiving Party without restriction prior to disclosure by the Disclosing Party; (ii) became publicly available through no act or omission of the Receiving Party; (iii) is received from a third party without breach of any obligation owed to the Disclosing Party; or (iv) was developed at any time by the Receiving Party independent of the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.2. The confidentiality obligations set forth under this Agreement do not extend to Excluded Information. For the purposes of this Agreement, “Excluded Information” *may* mean information that the Receiving Party *asserts*, (i) was allegedly known to the Receiving Party; (ii) allegedly became publicly available; (iii) allegedly is received from a third party; or (iv) was allegedly developed by the Receiving Party. All assertions must be submitted to arbitration.
perturbation.explanation: The original 'Excluded Information' clause provides clear conditions under which information is not considered confidential. The changed clause introduces subjectivity and arbitration, undermining the clarity. The word 'demonstrate' is replaced with 'asserts', making this change. This affects the enforcement of confidentiality since the Receiving Party needs to 'assert' instead of 'demonstrate' their claim, creating contradiction within the text.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 6.5. Notwithstanding anything else in this Agreement, the Receiving Party’s obligations of confidentiality and restricted use hereunder shall continue in force during the term of this Agreement and shall survive for three (3) years following the expiration or termination of this Agreement or, in the case of trade secrets, until such time as the Confidential Information does not qualify as a trade secret.
perturbation.justification: The original text in section 1.2 provides objective criteria ('demonstrate') for determining 'Excluded Information', which isn't subject to confidentiality. The changed text introduces subjectivity ('asserts', 'allegedly') and mandatory arbitration. This directly contradicts section 6.5, which states the confidentiality obligations 'shall continue in force'. The change undermines the clear and continuous obligation by making excluded information dependent on potentially contested assertions and arbitration, creating a strong in-text contradiction as per the 'Ambiguities - In Text Contradiction' definition. Because the original provides an objective measure, but the change creates an ambiguity, it strongly breaks the compatability with the contradicted section. By switching 'demonstrates' to 'asserts' a strong contradiction is created.

perturbation.original_text: 2. RESTRICTED USE 2.1. The Receiving Party must keep Confidential Information under its control, establish and maintain adequate security measures to safeguard Confidential Information and shall treat the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information with at least the same degree of care as it protects its own confidential and proprietary information of a similar nature but, in any event, with no less than a reasonable degree of care. The Receiving Party shall only use the Confidential Information for the Disclosure Purpose.
perturbation.changed_text: 2. RESTRICTED USE 2.1. The Receiving Party should endeavor to keep Confidential Information under its control, and should try to establish and maintain some security measures to safeguard Confidential Information and should attempt to treat the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information with some degree of care. The Receiving Party shall predominantly use the Confidential Information for the Disclosure Purpose.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates specific actions with strict standards of care. The changed text weakens these obligations with words like 'endeavor,' 'try,' and 'attempt,' making the section non-binding. 'Predominantly use' allows non-disclosure purposes. This directly contradicts the original intent of restricted use. In the term 'should' in the changed text, make the contract less binding.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.2. The Receiving Party shall not disclose, duplicate, transmit or otherwise disseminate in any manner the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information, except to the Receiving Party’s officers, directors and employees (collectively “Employees”), and the Receiving Party’s agents, contractors and advisors (including, without limitation, financial advisors, legal advisors, auditors and accountants) retained by the Receiving Party (collectively “Advisors”). All such persons receiving Confidential Information shall have a need to know and shall be bound by professional duty or in writing (in advance) to confidentiality MUTUAL NON-DISCLOSURE AGREEMENT No Reservations Confidential Page 2 of 3 No Reservations (Mutual) NDA v01 and non-use obligations at least as protective of such information as this Agreement. The Receiving Party shall be liable to the Disclosing Party in the event that any of its Employees or Advisors breach such obligations, and upon the Disclosing Party’s request, the Receiving Party shall promptly provide documentary proof of its compliance with the immediately preceding sentence.
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the original obligations regarding confidential information handling by using terms like 'endeavor,' 'try,' 'attempt,' and 'predominantly.' This introduces ambiguity and makes the obligations non-binding. This directly contradicts section 2.2, which mandates that the Receiving Party 'shall not disclose' confidential information and that all recipients 'shall be bound' by confidentiality obligations. The weakened language in the changed text allows for potential disclosures or a lower standard of care, directly conflicting with the strict requirements of section 2.2.

perturbation.original_text: 2.2. The Receiving Party shall not disclose, duplicate, transmit or otherwise disseminate in any manner the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information, except to the Receiving Party’s officers, directors and employees (collectively “Employees”), and the Receiving Party’s agents, contractors and advisors (including, without limitation, financial advisors, legal advisors, auditors and accountants) retained by the Receiving Party (collectively “Advisors”). All such persons receiving Confidential Information shall have a need to know and shall be bound by professional duty or in writing (in advance) to confidentiality MUTUAL NON-DISCLOSURE AGREEMENT No Reservations Confidential Page 2 of 3 No Reservations (Mutual) NDA v01 and non-use obligations at least as protective of such information as this Agreement. The Receiving Party shall be liable to the Disclosing Party in the event that any of its Employees or Advisors breach such obligations, and upon the Disclosing Party’s request, the Receiving Party shall promptly provide documentary proof of its compliance with the immediately preceding sentence.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.2. The Receiving Party should aim to prevent the transmission of Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information, except to the Receiving Party’s employees (collectively “Employees”), and the Receiving Party’s advisors (including legal advisors) retained by the Receiving Party (collectively “Advisors”). All such persons receiving Confidential Information should preferably have a need to know and be encouraged to sign a confidentiality agreement. The Receiving Party may not be liable to the Disclosing Party if its Employees or Advisors breach such obligations.
perturbation.explanation: This section introduces ambiguity by softening the restrictions on disclosure and liability. The original text uses 'shall not disclose', creating a strict prohibition, while the modified version uses 'should aim to prevent', which is much weaker. The original text requires individuals receiving Confidential Information to be 'bound by professional duty or in writing'. The revised text states that individuals 'should preferably have a need to know and be encouraged to sign a confidentiality agreement.' This weakens the binding nature of the agreement. There is a direct contradiction because the original text states 'The Receiving Party shall be liable to the Disclosing Party in the event that any of its Employees or Advisors breach such obligations,' while the revised text states 'The Receiving Party may not be liable to the Disclosing Party if its Employees or Advisors breach such obligations.'
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5.2. The unauthorized disclosure or use of any Confidential Information may cause immediate and irreparable injury to the Disclosing Party which could not be adequately compensated by monetary damages. Each party therefore authorizes the other party to seek any temporary or permanent injunctive relief necessary to prevent such disclosure or use, or threat of disclosure or use.
perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly weakens the confidentiality obligations and introduces ambiguity regarding liability, directly contradicting the original text. This undermines the clause in section 5.2, which asserts that unauthorized disclosure will cause 'immediate and irreparable injury' and allows for injunctive relief. The original text ensures strict confidentiality and liability for breaches, whereas the changed text provides weaker assurances and potential non-liability, making it unclear if injunctive relief would be applicable or justified under the new terms. The change introduces ambiguity where the original text and section 5.2 expect certainty, thus fulfilling the condition for a strong contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 3.1. As between the Disclosing Party and the Receiving Party, Confidential Information and all applicable intellectual property rights embodied in the Confidential Information shall remain the sole property of the Disclosing Party. Nothing in this Agreement shall be construed as granting any rights to the Receiving Party or any of its Employees or Advisors, by license or otherwise, to any of the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information, except to the extent as expressly stated in this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.1. As between the Disclosing Party and the Receiving Party, Confidential Information and all applicable intellectual property rights embodied in the Confidential Information shall generally remain the property of the Disclosing Party unless the Receiving Party can prove independent development. Nothing in this Agreement shall be construed as granting any rights to the Receiving Party or any of its Employees or Advisors, except to the extent as implied.
perturbation.explanation: The modified clause introduces uncertainty regarding ownership of intellectual property. The original text clearly states that intellectual property rights remain the 'sole' property of the Disclosing Party. The added provision allows the Receiving Party to potentially claim ownership by proving 'independent development,' creating an exception that wasn't there before. The change from 'expressly stated' to 'implied' further creates potential disputes over rights. This affects the section that allows the disclosing party to seek injunctive relief.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5.2. The unauthorized disclosure or use of any Confidential Information may cause immediate and irreparable injury to the Disclosing Party which could not be adequately compensated by monetary damages. Each party therefore authorizes the other party to seek any temporary or permanent injunctive relief necessary to prevent such disclosure or use, or threat of disclosure or use.
perturbation.justification: The original text states that Confidential Information remains the 'sole property' of the Disclosing Party. The changed text introduces an exception where the Receiving Party can claim ownership by proving 'independent development'. This directly contradicts the 'sole property' clause. Further, the shift from 'expressly stated' to 'implied' introduces ambiguity regarding permitted uses of Confidential Information. This ambiguity weakens the Disclosing Party's ability to seek injunctive relief as outlined in section 5.2, because proving unauthorized use becomes more difficult when rights are implied rather than expressly stated. Therefore, the modified clause creates a strong in-text contradiction with the contradicted text.

perturbation.original_text: 6.3. Upon expiration or termination of this Agreement the Receiving Party's right to use the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information ceases and the Receiving Party must, upon the Disclosing Party’s request and at the Receiving Party’s expense, promptly return to the Disclosing Party or permanently destroy all of the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information in its power, possession or control. Having acknowledged that it, particularly within a networked environment, the return and/or destruction of electronically stored information may be impossible, extremely or otherwise unduly difficult or costly, the immediately preceding sentence is not intended to, and does not obligate the Receiving Party to make extra-ordinary efforts to destroy electronically stored copies of the Confidential Information, provided that, in relation to any such copy that is not destroyed the Receiving Party’s obligations of confidentiality and restricted use shall continue as per the provisions of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 6.3. Upon expiration or termination of this Agreement the Receiving Party's right to use the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information ceases, and the Receiving Party should, if practical, return to the Disclosing Party or destroy all of the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information in its possession. The Receiving Party is not required to make efforts to destroy electronically stored copies of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause mandates the return or destruction of confidential information upon termination. The modified version weakens this obligation by adding qualifiers like 'if practical' and removing the 'promptly' requirement. This creates a conflict. The change from 'must' to 'should' weakens the contract and allows them to avoid doing their duties.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 6.4. Notwithstanding Section 6.3, the Receiving Party may retain a reasonable number of copies of the Confidential Information (and any materials embedding the same) (“Retained Copies”) for the sole purpose of (i) satisfying any legal or regulatory requirements regarding record and data retention that the Receiving Party is obligated to comply with; (ii) enforcing this Agreement; and/or (iii) archiving consistent with good business practices and the Receiving Party’s internal policies. For the avoidance of doubt, the Receiving Party’s obligations of confidentiality and restricted use as set forth in this Agreement shall continue in respect of such Retained Copies as per the provisions of this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates the return or destruction of confidential information upon termination ('must'). The changed version weakens this obligation by adding qualifiers like 'if practical' and changing 'must' to 'should'. Section 6.4, the contradicted text, allows the Receiving Party to retain copies for specific purposes, implying a clear obligation to destroy all other copies per 6.3. The weakened obligation in the changed 6.3 now contradicts the underlying assumption of 6.4 that destruction is the default action unless an exception applies. This introduces ambiguity and inconsistency, thus creating a contradiction.
